raw_case:
Pentalogy of Cantrell Marked With Ectopia Cordis, Gastroschisis, and Cystic Hygroma in the First Trimester: A Rare Case
Pentalogy of Cantrell is a rare congenital syndrome characterized by defects in the abdominal wall, sternum, diaphragm, and heart. A severe manifestation of this syndrome is ectopia cordis, where the heart is located partially or entirely outside the chest cavity. Gastroschisis involves a defect in the abdominal wall, where the intestines protrude outside the abdomen without a protective membrane. Cystic hygroma is a malformation of the lymphatic system leading to fluid-filled cysts. We present the case of a 29-year-old G3P0020 female initially seen for obstetric follow-up due to a threatened abortion at 11 weeks of gestation with vaginal bleeding. During a routine limited outpatient ultrasound at 11 weeks and four days of gestation, the heart was found to be entirely extrathoracic. Further ultrasound imaging confirmed the presence of ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma. Despite the poor prognosis and high risk of intrauterine fetal demise, the patient was referred to a higher-level care facility. This case highlights the critical role of first-trimester ultrasound in diagnosing severe fetal anomalies and the importance of early recognition and prompt referral for the best possible outcomes.

Introduction

Ectopia cordis is an exceptionally rare congenital anomaly characterized by the partial or complete displacement of the heart outside the thoracic cavity. Its estimated annual incidence ranges from 5.5 to 7.9 per million live births, with detection possible as early as the first trimester using transabdominal ultrasound [1]. Some studies suggest that first-trimester ultrasound has a sensitivity as high as 93.26% for detecting ectopia cordis [2]. This condition is often associated with pentalogy of Cantrell, a syndrome involving five congenital defects affecting the sternum, pericardium, diaphragm, anterior abdominal wall, and heart [3]. The prognosis for ectopia cordis is generally poor. In a study of 31 cases, 15 pregnancies were terminated, seven resulted in spontaneous fetal demise, and only seven fetuses survived to birth. Notably, of those who survived, all three who underwent surgical correction survived [1]. This emphasizes the critical role of surgical intervention in the management of ectopia cordis.Gastroschisis is a congenital defect of the abdominal wall where the bowel protrudes through the defect without a protective membrane. Infants with gastroschisis, of whom up to 80% are born prematurely with an average gestational age of 36 weeks, are at high risk for growth failure, with 15% also experiencing intrauterine growth restriction [4]. Notably, like ectopia cordis, gastroschisis has been described as one of the potential defects in pentalogy of Cantrell [5]. The condition is often correlated with elevated maternal levels of alpha-fetoprotein. Prompt surgical intervention after birth can result in a favorable survival rate for neonates with gastroschisis [6]. Infants born with this condition tend to have better outcomes when delivered at facilities equipped with pediatric surgeons, high-risk obstetricians, and neonatologists, ensuring comprehensive care from an advanced interprofessional team [4].Cystic hygroma is a vascular anomaly characterized by malformation of the lymphatic structures, most commonly occurring in the neck. When detected in utero, the prognosis is particularly poor, especially when accompanied by other abnormalities, such as cardiac defects. Cystic hygroma is strongly associated with aneuploidies, including Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome), Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome), and Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) [7]. In our review of the literature, we found no reports of a first-trimester fetus presenting with the simultaneous occurrence of all three anomalies: ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma.

Case presentation

A 29-year-old African American female, gravida 3 para 0-0-2-0 (G3P0020), presented at 11 weeks and four days of gestation for a routine obstetric visit and limited ultrasound. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, two prior spontaneous abortions, a BMI over 50, and insomnia. She has had two previous dilatation and curettage procedures. Her family history is significant for diabetes mellitus in both her mother and sister. The patient is currently taking prenatal vitamins and melatonin. She reported that all three pregnancies were with the same partner. One month prior to this visit, the patient was diagnosed with cystitis and trichomoniasis after presenting to the emergency department (ED) with dysuria. A positive urine beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (β-HCG) test confirmed the pregnancy, and she was prescribed metronidazole (Flagyl) and cephalexin (Keflex). Urinalysis revealed urobilinogen, blood, ketones, protein, nitrites, leukocyte esterase, epithelial cells, white blood cells, red blood cells, bacteria, and trichomonas (Table 1). Based on her last menstrual period, she was estimated to be at seven weeks and one day of gestation at that time. She followed up with her obstetrician/gynecologist (Ob/Gyn) a couple of days later to establish prenatal care, where an ultrasound confirmed an intrauterine pregnancy without congenital anomalies (Figure 1). Three days following this visit, she experienced vaginal bleeding and was diagnosed with a threatened abortion. On follow-up with her Ob/Gyn, she was found to have a closed cervix, a viable intrauterine pregnancy, and resolved bleeding, with no further complaints.Prenatal screening, including non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT), showed no abnormalities (Table 2), and the patient's thyroid-stimulating hormone level was 1.14, indicating well-controlled hypothyroidism. At 11 weeks and four days of gestation, a transvaginal ultrasound suggested the presence of severe congenital abnormalities, leading to a referral to a local hospital for further radiological evaluation and an official radiology report.Ultrasound determined the fetal heart rate to be 174 beats per minute (bpm). An abnormal appearance of the fetus was noted, including a hypoechoic region along the fetal head and neck, raising concern for cystic hygroma (Figure 2). Additionally, echogenic material was observed outside the fetus, likely representing fetal viscera, which suggested a developmental anomaly with a body wall defect, such as gastroschisis (Figure 3). Furthermore, the fetal heart also appeared to be positioned externally (Figure 4 and Video 1).At 13 weeks, a follow-up transvaginal ultrasound detected a fetal heart rate of 157 bpm. The patient was then informed about the presence of multiple severe congenital anomalies and was advised of the high likelihood of fetal demise. She was referred to a maternal-fetal medicine specialist and a highly equipped multidisciplinary care center for a comprehensive evaluation and management.

Discussion

Ectopia cordis can be classified into four subtypes based on the anatomical location of the heart: thoracic, thoracic-abdominal, abdominal, and cervical. The thoracic type, in which the heart protrudes outside the thoracic cavity without any overlying structures, is the most common. The thoracic-abdominal type, characterized by the heart's protrusion involving both the thoracic and abdominal cavities, is most frequently associated with the rare congenital anomaly known as pentalogy of Cantrell [8,9]. Our case aligns with this thoracic-abdominal type. The exact etiology of ectopia cordis remains unclear, but it is believed to involve abnormalities in ventral folding morphogenesis, a process crucial for the development of the heart tube and closure of the ventral body wall. Various theories have been proposed, including the arrest of descent of the lateral body folds, rupture of the chorion leading to failure of midline fusion, and the involvement of amniotic bands, which together could result in the severe malformations characteristic of Cantrell's syndrome, such as the heart protruding outside the chest. Recent studies have suggested a link between alterations in bone morphogenic protein 2 (BMP-2) expression and ventral wall defects that can lead to ectopia cordis [8]. In our case, the patient presented with a threatened abortion during the early stages of pregnancy, which may have contributed to this developmental defect. The potential insult during this critical period could have disrupted normal embryological processes, leading to the malformation.A study examining outcomes for ectopia cordis found that 66% of the cohort were classified as very-low-birth-weight infants. Among these infants, only 6% either survived to discharge or were still alive after one year of hospitalization and inpatient treatment. In contrast, 41% of non-very-low-birth-weight infants in the same patient population met the same survival criteria [10]. These findings suggest that while the overall prognosis for ectopia cordis remains poor, ensuring adequate nutrition in utero to prevent very-low-birth-weight infants might improve outcomes. Future research could focus on strategies to optimize prenatal nutrition and care to promote better survival rates for these infants. For more detailed evaluation following a routine obstetric ultrasound, clinicians could consider fetal echocardiograms to assess for additional congenital heart diseases, such as tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus, double-outlet right ventricle, or ventricular septal defects. Unfortunately, in our case, a fetal echocardiogram was not available due to local resource limitations [1].The prognosis and outcomes for gastroschisis are complex and highly variable, depending on whether it is an isolated anomaly or accompanied by other anomalies. Infants with additional anomalies, particularly congenital cardiac anomalies like our patient, often have significantly worse outcomes and account for the majority of in utero fetal demises [11]. Compared to neonates born without abdominal wall defects, those with gastroschisis face a poor prognosis when complications such as sepsis, necrotizing enterocolitis, or short bowel syndrome occur [11,12]. A literature review highlighted that in studies analyzed, 80% of infants with gastroschisis underwent immediate surgery. Of these, 69% had primary closure performed by manually returning the bowel to the abdominal cavity, while 31% underwent repair using a sterile silo-based method [12]. The risk factors for the development of gastroschisis are not well understood, but there is evidence of a correlation with genitourinary infections acquired before or during the first trimester. This aligns with our patient's case, as they tested positive for cystitis-induced trichomoniasis on the day the pregnancy was first identified [6].Cystic hygroma, also known as nuchal edema, most commonly occurs in the neck region, accounting for 75% of cases, as observed in our patient. The axilla is the second most common site, representing 20% of cases [7]. About half of cystic hygromas are associated with aneuploidies, including Turner syndrome and Trisomy 21, the latter being the most frequently associated. There is also a known correlation with DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion) [7]. Cystic hygroma has been identified in other cases presenting simultaneously with pentalogy of Cantrell. For instance, a case report described twin fetuses both presenting with cystic hygroma and congenital defects associated with Cantrell’s syndrome, detected via ultrasound. In that case, the defects were first identified at 10 weeks and five days of gestation, whereas in our case, anomalies were recognized at 12 weeks and one day of gestation [13]. When cystic hygroma is the sole anomaly and the karyotype is normal, the size of the hygroma can be a useful prognostic indicator. A nuchal size greater than 6.5 mm is associated with a poorer prognosis, while a size less than 6 mm is associated with a more favorable prognosis [7]. However, in our case, we did not use this measurement due to the presence of other anomalies that precluded an isolated prognostic assessment, along with the possibility of spontaneous resolution of cystic hygroma in utero [14].In cases of suspected pentalogy of Cantrell, the first indication may come from a routine ultrasound performed in the late first trimester [1]. Clinicians should be aware that pentalogy of Cantrell can be classified into three categories: Class 1 is a definitive diagnosis involving all five defects, Class 2 is a probable diagnosis with at least four defects, and Class 3 represents an incomplete expression with some of the defects present [9]. Due to resource limitations, our case falls under a Class 3 categorization. However, when available, fetal echocardiography or fetal MRI can be used to further characterize defects prenatally and assess their severity [1,9,15]. If the fetus survives, further evaluation of the defects can be conducted in the postnatal period using various modalities, including chest X-rays, echocardiography, cardiac catheterization, CT angiography, and surgical assessment [15]. When an anomaly is detected on prenatal ultrasound, we recommend non-invasive prenatal testing, which has high sensitivity and specificity for detecting common aneuploidies and chromosomal deletions through the analysis of cell-free fetal DNA in maternal circulation [16]. Clinicians should also consider that pentalogy of Cantrell and cystic hygroma are strongly associated with Trisomy 21, Trisomy 18, and Turner syndrome, each of which carries significant prognostic implications [7,16]. Importantly, if a fetus with ectopia cordis survives to birth, birth weight is a key prognostic factor [10]. Given the complex nature of this condition, delivery should be planned at a well-equipped facility with a multidisciplinary team specialized in handling such cases, as surgery is crucial for survival. As soon as pentalogy of Cantrell is suspected, prompt referral to a comprehensive care center is essential [1,12,15].

Conclusions

This case highlights the rare occurrence of pentalogy of Cantrell, particularly through the detection of ectopia cordis, cystic hygroma, and gastroschisis, representing a novel presentation of the syndrome. It emphasizes the critical role of routine prenatal ultrasound in detecting anatomical anomalies, which should be followed by a thorough evaluation by obstetricians. Early ultrasound and appropriate prenatal screenings can identify pentalogy of Cantrell as early as the first trimester, especially in cases with severe malformations. Clinicians should recognize that pentalogy of Cantrell can present with a broad spectrum of defects, beyond those observed in this case. The complexity of pentalogy of Cantrell necessitates a coordinated, multidisciplinary approach to evaluation and treatment planning. Prenatal planning should include surgical consultation, as timely surgical intervention remains a crucial option for infants born with pentalogy of Cantrell, particularly in cases involving ectopia cordis. Future research should focus on identifying maternal risk factors contributing to the development of pentalogy of Cantrell and exploring innovative strategies to prevent in-utero and postnatal fetal demise.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 29-year-old African American female, Gravida 3 Para 0-0-2-0
- Chief Complaint: None (Routine obstetric follow-up with ultrasound at 11 weeks and 4 days gestation).
- History of Present Illness: Confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy with vaginal bleeding in earlier weeks, diagnosed with threatened abortion.
- Past Medical History: Hypothyroidism, two prior spontaneous abortions, BMI > 50, insomnia, prior cystitis, and trichomoniasis.
- Personal History: Prenatal vitamins and melatonin use.
- Family History: Diabetes mellitus in mother and sister.
- Physical Examination: None provided.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Initial ultrasound detected no congenital abnormalities.
  - Transvaginal ultrasound at 11 weeks and 4 days confirmed severe congenital abnormalities:
    - Ectopia cordis: Heart located outside thoracic cavity.
    - Gastroschisis: Fetal viscera extruded through body wall defect.
    - Cystic hygroma: Hypoechoic region indicating lymphatic malformation in the neck.
  - Fetal heart rate measured as 174 bpm (11 weeks 4 days) and 157 bpm (13 weeks).
  - Prenatal screening, including non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT), returned no abnormalities.
  - Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 1.14, consistent with controlled hypothyroidism.
  - Urinalysis detected bacteria and trichomonas prior to pregnancy confirmation.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Pentalogy of Cantrell (Definitive Diagnosis):**
  - Based on evidence of multiple congenital anomalies (ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, cystic hygroma).
  - Fits the classification of complex pentalogy of Cantrell, likely Class 3 (incomplete expression with multiple defects).

- **Isolated Ectopia Cordis:**
  - Excluded due to the concomitant presence of abdominal wall defect (gastroschisis) and cystic hygroma.

- **Cystic Hygroma Associated with Aneuploidy:**
  - Excluded as non-invasive prenatal testing showed no chromosomal abnormalities.

- **Gastroschisis as an Isolated Defect:**
  - Excluded as it occurred in conjunction with ectopia cordis and cystic hygroma.

- **Other Syndromic Abnormalities (e.g., Turner Syndrome, Trisomy 21, Trisomy 18):**
  - Excluded due to absence of genetic abnormalities from prenatal screening and atypical presentation.

Ultrasound findings and the constellation of signs collectively confirmed a diagnosis involving multiple congenital defects, aligning with pentalogy of Cantrell.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Pentalogy of Cantrell with ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma (Class 3 incomplete expression).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Pentalogy of Cantrell with ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- Patient Information: 29-year-old African American female, Gravida 3 Para 0-0-2-0
- Chief Complaint: None (Routine obstetric follow-up with ultrasound at 11 weeks and 4 days gestation).
- History of Present Illness: Confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy with vaginal bleeding in earlier weeks, diagnosed with threatened abortion.
- Past Medical History: Hypothyroidism, two prior spontaneous abortions, BMI > 50, insomnia, prior cystitis, and trichomoniasis.
- Personal History: Prenatal vitamins and melatonin use.
- Family History: Diabetes mellitus in mother and sister.
- Physical Examination: None provided.
- Ancillary Tests:
  - Initial ultrasound detected no congenital abnormalities.
  - Transvaginal ultrasound at 11 weeks and 4 days confirmed severe congenital abnormalities:
    - Ectopia cordis: Heart located outside thoracic cavity.
    - Gastroschisis: Fetal viscera extruded through body wall defect.
    - Cystic hygroma: Hypoechoic region indicating lymphatic malformation in the neck.
  - Fetal heart rate measured as 174 bpm (11 weeks 4 days) and 157 bpm (13 weeks).
  - Prenatal screening, including non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT), returned no abnormalities.
  - Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 1.14, consistent with controlled hypothyroidism.
  - Urinalysis detected bacteria and trichomonas prior to pregnancy confirmation.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
### Reasoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 29-year-old African American female, Gravida 3 Para 0-0-2-0, indicating three pregnancies resulting in no term births, no preterm births, two spontaneous abortions, and no living children. This history of recurrent pregnancy loss is noted.
<step 2> The chief complaint is absent, but the report focuses on a routine obstetric follow-up at 11 weeks and 4 days gestation, with a history of threatened abortion earlier in the pregnancy.
<step 3> Key findings are from the transvaginal ultrasound at 11 weeks and 4 days, which revealed severe congenital abnormalities: ectopia cordis (heart outside the chest), gastroschisis (abdominal wall defect with extruded viscera), and cystic hygroma (lymphatic malformation in the neck). An initial ultrasound had not detected these abnormalities, suggesting their development or increased visibility occurred between scans.
<step 4> Ancillary tests include NIPT (non-invasive prenatal testing), which returned no abnormalities. This result is discordant with the ultrasound findings of multiple severe structural anomalies. While NIPT is a screening tool with high sensitivity for common aneuploidies, it is not diagnostic and can yield false negatives, especially for certain conditions or if fetal fraction is low.
<step 5> Other tests indicate controlled hypothyroidism (TSH 1.14), and a history of urinary tract issues (bacteria, trichomonas) prior to pregnancy confirmation, which are maternal health factors but not directly causative of the fetal anomalies observed. Maternal BMI > 50 is a general risk factor for pregnancy complications.
<step 6> The constellation of severe congenital anomalies – ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma – is highly concerning. Ectopia cordis and cystic hygroma, in particular, are strongly associated with genetic syndromes and chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Trisomies 13, 18, 21, Turner syndrome). Gastroschisis is typically considered an isolated defect, but its co-occurrence with other major anomalies suggests a more complex underlying etiology.
<step 7> Despite the negative NIPT result, the ultrasound findings of multiple severe structural malformations are definitive evidence of a significant fetal condition. The NIPT result does not negate the observable abnormalities. The primary diagnostic conclusion must reflect these profound structural defects. Given the severity and combination of anomalies, the prognosis is extremely poor, and these findings are indicative of a severe congenital malformation syndrome or a significant developmental anomaly that is likely incompatible with long-term survival. Further invasive genetic testing (amniocentesis for karyotype and chromosomal microarray) would typically be recommended to ascertain the specific genetic cause, but based solely on the provided information, the diagnosis is the presence of these severe malformations.

### Answer:
Fetus with Ectopia Cordis, Gastroschisis, and Cystic Hygroma

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 29-year-old African American female, Gravida 3 Para 0-0-2-0, indicating three pregnancies resulting in no term births, no preterm births, two spontaneous abortions, and no living children. This history of recurrent pregnancy loss is noted.
The chief complaint is absent, but the report focuses on a routine obstetric follow-up at 11 weeks and 4 days gestation, with a history of threatened abortion earlier in the pregnancy.

Key findings are from the transvaginal ultrasound at 11 weeks and 4 days, which revealed severe congenital abnormalities: ectopia cordis (heart outside the chest), gastroschisis (abdominal wall defect with extruded viscera), and cystic hygroma (lymphatic malformation in the neck). An initial ultrasound had not detected these abnormalities, suggesting their development or increased visibility occurred between scans.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Ectopia cordis, Gastroschisis, Cystic hygroma, Transvaginal ultrasound, Congenital abnormalities, Fetal development timeline, Prenatal ultrasound detection
judgment_path.info: Pentalogy of Cantrell is a rare congenital syndrome that includes severe anomalies such as ectopia cordis (where the heart is outside the thoracic cavity), gastroschisis (a defect in the abdominal wall allowing intestine protrusion), and cystic hygroma (a fluid-filled cyst due to lymphatic malformation). Symptoms are diagnosed through early ultrasound imaging, which can identify these defects as early as the first trimester, indicated by abnormal fetal positions and structures. In a reported case, a 29-year-old woman at 11 weeks of gestation experienced vaginal bleeding, and subsequent ultrasounds revealed the presence of these severe congenital abnormalities, leading to high risks of intrauterine fetal demise. Early detection through ultrasound is critical for timely referrals and management, often necessitating a multidisciplinary approach for better outcomes.

reasoning_eval.step: Ancillary tests include NIPT (non-invasive prenatal testing), which returned no abnormalities. This result is discordant with the ultrasound findings of multiple severe structural anomalies. While NIPT is a screening tool with high sensitivity for common aneuploidies, it is not diagnostic and can yield false negatives, especially for certain conditions or if fetal fraction is low.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: NIPT sensitivity, NIPT false negatives, Fetal fraction in NIPT, Ultrasound findings, NIPT limitations, Structural anomalies in ultrasound, NIPT diagnostic accuracy
judgment_path.info:
无创DNA产前检测（NIPT）是一种通过分析孕妇静脉血中胎儿游离DNA来检测胎儿是否患有染色体疾病（如唐氏综合症、爱德华综合症、帕陶综合症）的技术。该检测适用于高风险人群，例如年龄超过35岁的孕妇、超声结果显示非整倍体高危的孕妇及过去生育过三体患儿的孕妇。检测结果通常在10个工作日内可得，准确率超过90%。尽管该技术无创伤性并能降低流产和感染风险，但对一些特定人群（如有行为影响的疾病、重度肥胖、以及多胎妊娠）准确性可能下降。适合的孕妇需在充分知情同意的基础上进行检测。

无创产前基因检测（NIPT）是一种通过分析母体血液中的游离DNA，在妊娠早期（最早在孕10周）筛查包括21三体、18三体和13三体综合征在内的常见染色体异常的技术。该检测的敏感性和特异性均达到99%以上，且相较于其他筛查方案具有更高的检出率和更低的假阳性率。NIPT惟是一种筛查工具，结果并不能作为诊断依据，因此需进行后续确认性检测以排除假阳性或假阴性情况。尽管其有效性高，但也有一定的局限性，包括不筛查多倍体及某些出生缺陷。

reasoning_eval.step: Other tests indicate controlled hypothyroidism (TSH 1.14), and a history of urinary tract issues (bacteria, trichomonas) prior to pregnancy confirmation, which are maternal health factors but not directly causative of the fetal anomalies observed. Maternal BMI > 50 is a general risk factor for pregnancy complications.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: hypothyroidism in pregnancy, TSH levels in pregnancy, maternal BMI and pregnancy risks, bacterial infections and pregnancy, trichomonas infection in pregnancy, fetal anomalies causes, maternal health and fetal development
judgment_path.info:
Hypothyroidism during pregnancy is commonly caused by autoimmune disorders like Hashimoto's thyroiditis, or can result from untreated or over-treated thyroid conditions. Symptoms may include a TSH level greater than 6 mIU/L, with 0.4% of women experiencing levels greater than 10 mIU/L. Diagnosis is based on thyroid function tests, which should remain within trimester-specific normal ranges. An enlarged thyroid gland, or goiter, may also occur, particularly in iodine-deficient areas, but is rare in the U.S. Monitoring and appropriate treatment of hypothyroidism during pregnancy are crucial to prevent risks for both the mother and the baby.

The management of thyroid disorders during pregnancy involves diagnosing thyroid dysfunction using trimester- and manufacturer-specific reference ranges for serum thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) and free thyroxine (fT4). Key symptoms of thyroid dysfunction include abnormal TSH levels, with overt hypothyroidism presenting as increased TSH and decreased fT4, while subclinical hypothyroidism (SCH) features increased TSH with normal fT4. Diagnosis should ideally occur early in pregnancy, especially for those with known risk factors like previous thyroid disorders or autoimmune conditions. For women on levothyroxine, frequent monitoring of TSH and fT4 levels is essential to ensure they remain within targeted ranges to prevent adverse pregnancy outcomes. Treatment recommendations also suggest adjusting levothyroxine dosage upon confirming pregnancy to meet increased hormonal needs and reduce risks of complications such as miscarriage and poor fetal development.

Hypothyroidism is the most prevalent thyroid disorder in pregnant women, affecting 3-5% of this population. It can manifest as subclinical or overt hypothyroidism, with subclinical hypothyroidism being more common. Diagnosis is often based on serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels that exceed pregnancy-specific reference ranges, with values above 2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester and above 3 mIU/L in subsequent trimesters indicating concern. Overt hypothyroidism is diagnosed with TSH levels over 10 mIU/L. Treatment with levothyroxine should be initiated promptly for those diagnosed with overt hypothyroidism to maintain TSH levels within normal ranges for pregnancy. The management of subclinical hypothyroidism varies due to inconsistent data regarding the benefits of treatment for maternal and child outcomes, leading most guidelines to suggest against universal screening. During pregnancy, many women already diagnosed with hypothyroidism may require an increase in their levothyroxine dosage.

reasoning_eval.step: The constellation of severe congenital anomalies – ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma – is highly concerning. Ectopia cordis and cystic hygroma, in particular, are strongly associated with genetic syndromes and chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Trisomies 13, 18, 21, Turner syndrome). Gastroschisis is typically considered an isolated defect, but its co-occurrence with other major anomalies suggests a more complex underlying etiology.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Ectopia cordis，Cystic hygroma，Gastroschisis，Congenital anomalies，Genetic syndromes，Chromosomal abnormalities，Trisomy 13，Trisomy 18，Trisomy 21，Turner syndrome，Isolated gastroschisis
judgment_path.info: Pentalogy of Cantrell is a rare congenital syndrome marked by defects in the abdominal wall, sternum, diaphragm, and heart, with severe cases presenting as ectopia cordis, where the heart is found outside the thoracic cavity. Additionally, gastroschisis, where the intestines protrude outside the abdomen, and cystic hygroma, characterized by fluid-filled cysts from lymphatic malformation, may also occur. Early diagnosis during the first trimester often utilizes ultrasound imaging, revealing the presence of these anomalies, alongside symptoms such as vaginal bleeding in the mother indicative of a threatened abortion. Due to the associated high risks, including intrauterine fetal demise, timely referral for advanced care is essential. This underscores the importance of first-trimester screening to improve outcomes in affected pregnancies, although prognosis remains poor for cases with multiple congenital defects.

reasoning_eval.step: Despite the negative NIPT result, the ultrasound findings of multiple severe structural malformations are definitive evidence of a significant fetal condition. The NIPT result does not negate the observable abnormalities. The primary diagnostic conclusion must reflect these profound structural defects. Given the severity and combination of anomalies, the prognosis is extremely poor, and these findings are indicative of a severe congenital malformation syndrome or a significant developmental anomaly that is likely incompatible with long-term survival. Further invasive genetic testing (amniocentesis for karyotype and chromosomal microarray) would typically be recommended to ascertain the specific genetic cause, but based solely on the provided information, the diagnosis is the presence of these severe malformations.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: NIPT，negative predictive value of NIPT，fetal structural malformations，ultrasound abnormalities，severe congenital malformation syndrome，developmental anomaly prognosis，amniocentesis，karyotype analysis，chromosomal microarray，genetic testing for congenital anomalies
judgment_path.info: The retrospective study analyzed the effectiveness of Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT) and an enhanced version termed NIPT plus, in detecting fetal chromosomal abnormalities, particularly in pregnant women categorized as high-risk due to factors such as age or previous abnormal findings. The study demonstrated that combining NIPT plus with ultrasound significantly enhances the accurate detection of complex chromosomal conditions like sex chromosome aneuploidies (SCA), microdeletion/microduplication syndromes (MMS), and rare autosomal trisomies (RAT), achieving a sensitivity of 88.24% for these rare conditions. In contrast, NIPT alone exhibited higher false negative rates, particularly for SCA, MMS, and RAT, underscoring its limitations. The diagnostic performance of NIPT plus, while yielding more false positive results compared to standard NIPT, showed improved positive predictive value for common trisomies (T13, T18, T21) when combined with ultrasound. The findings support the implementation of NIPT plus alongside ultrasound as an optimal screening strategy, particularly for complicated chromosomal abnormalities, reinforcing the need for comprehensive prenatal screening protocols.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The definitive diagnosis of Pentalogy of Cantrell was based on evidence of multiple congenital anomalies, including ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma.
<Step 2> Pentalogy of Cantrell fits the classification of Class 3, indicating incomplete expression with multiple defects.
<Step 3> Isolated ectopia cordis was excluded due to the concomitant presence of gastroschisis and cystic hygroma.
<Step 4> Cystic hygroma associated with aneuploidy was excluded as non-invasive prenatal testing showed no chromosomal abnormalities.
<Step 5> Gastroschisis as an isolated defect was excluded because it occurred in conjunction with ectopia cordis and cystic hygroma.

<Step 6> Other syndromic abnormalities, such as Turner syndrome, Trisomy 21, and Trisomy 18, were excluded due to the absence of genetic abnormalities found in prenatal screening and their atypical presentation.
<Step 7> Ultrasound findings and the constellation of signs collectively confirmed a diagnosis involving multiple congenital defects corresponding to Pentalogy of Cantrell.
<Step 8> Final diagnosis: Pentalogy of Cantrell with ectopia cordis, gastroschisis, and cystic hygroma, classified as Class 3 incomplete expression.
